does: This paper provides theoretical analysis of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) for language model post-training, proving that greedy sampling algorithms achieve provably efficient performance bounds when learning from preference feedback. The core contribution is establishing order-wise improvements over prior theoretical results by directly using empirical preference estimates rather than constructing optimistic or pessimistic estimates. The analysis covers both general preference models and the specialized Bradley-Terry model, demonstrating that greedy approaches are surprisingly sufficient despite their simplicity, leveraging the unique structural properties of the optimal policy class under KL-regularized objectives.
connects: This work builds on a growing literature establishing theoretical foundations for RLHF, extending beyond prior approaches that primarily studied reward-based Bradley-Terry preference models using optimism or pessimism principles borrowed from classical reinforcement learning theory. It responds to the gap between RLHF's empirical success in practice and limited theoretical understanding of learning from pairwise preferences with KL regularization. The paper connects to canonical RL theory but addresses the distinct challenges posed by preference feedback rather than direct reward signals, drawing on recent empirical observations about the practical relevance of general preference models beyond the Bradley-Terry assumption.
enables: These theoretical results enable better understanding of why practical RLHF systems succeed and could guide algorithm design by identifying that simple greedy approaches are theoretically justified rather than suboptimal heuristics. The work opens directions for further theoretical analysis of RLHF with different preference models, potential application of the structural insights to other feedback mechanisms in language model training, and development of algorithms that maintain theoretical guarantees while incorporating practical considerations. The findings may also inform hyperparameter selection and sampling strategies in real RLHF implementations, bridging the gap between theory and practice in language model post-training.
